summary: Gruber responds to speculation about missing default iPhone apps on the iPad (Stocks, Calculator, Clock, Weather, Voice Memos). He reveals that Apple internally had upscaled versions of these apps but Steve Jobs scrapped them because simply blowing up iPhone UIs to iPad size looked and felt wrong, even with higher-resolution graphics. He argues that the iPad running unmodified iPhone apps is actually un-Apple-like, representing a 'good enough' compromise rather than Apple's usual 'exactly right' standard. He predicts that non-optimized iPhone apps will quickly become the iPad's equivalent of Classic apps on Mac OS X — tolerated temporarily but eagerly replaced.
key_points: Apple had internal iPad versions of missing iPhone apps but Jobs killed them because upscaled UIs felt wrong on the larger screen
Running iPhone apps unmodified on the iPad is a rare Apple concession to compatibility over optimal user experience
A 3.5-inch screen and a 10-inch screen are fundamentally different design contexts, not just different sizes
iPhone games may translate better to iPad than non-game apps because gameplay doesn't depend as heavily on precise UI layout
Non-optimized iPhone apps will quickly become seen as stopgap measures users can't wait to replace with native iPad versions
money_quotes: It wasn't a technical problem, it was a design problem.
Ends up that just blowing up iPhone apps to fill the iPad screen looks and feels weird, even if you use higher-resolution graphics so that nothing looks pixelated.
So they were scrapped by you-know-who.
On the whole, it's actually rather un-Apple-like that they're even allowing iPhone apps to run unmodified on the iPad.
iPhone apps on the iPad are a "good enough" thing, not an "exactly right" thing.

Most companies — the ones that wouldn't even see it as a tough decision whether to allow iPhone apps to run on the iPad — settle for "good enough" all the time. Apple, on the other hand, usually goes for "exactly right".
A 3.5-inch screen is just totally different than a 10-inch screen.
I'm not saying it's a mistake that Apple is allowing the iPad to run iPhone apps. I'm just saying that the iPad is not a big iPhone.
key_insight: The iPad's ability to run iPhone apps is a pragmatic compatibility compromise that cuts against Apple's usual insistence on exactly-right design, because a larger screen demands fundamentally reimagined interfaces, not just scaled-up ones.
